justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Rolling deployment race condition at Step 4 clearly identified — instances split between old and new read sources with no coordination mechanism. (2) Missing backfill validation before Step 4 explicitly called out as an escalation and failure mode. (3) Non-resumable backfill identified with specific recommendation for cursor-based progress tracking. (4) Specific recommendations provided: validation query as deployment gate, feature flag for read-source switching, cursor-based resumable backfill with idempotent upserts. (5) Verdict is NOT READY. Additionally, Pippen went beyond the rubric by identifying the backfill-vs-dual-write race condition (stale data overwriting newer dual-write data), which is a genuine data-loss risk not explicitly in the expected behavior.

observations.text: Identified the backfill-vs-dual-write race condition causing silent data loss — a real and subtle bug beyond what the rubric required
Provided concrete SQL-level recommendations (INSERT ON CONFLICT with timestamp comparison) rather than vague guidance
Comprehensive observability review identifying the migration is essentially un-debuggable in production
Correctly escalated architectural gaps to MJ rather than overcorrecting by recommending a migration halt
Identified graceful degradation gap — read path should fall back to old table if new table row is missing
Called out Step 6 (DROP COLUMN) as irreversible and Step 5 as the point of no easy return — good operational awareness
Confidence level of 60 seems low given the thoroughness and correctness of the analysis — the identified issues are well-known migration antipatterns that Pippen correctly diagnosed
